graph_vector_fulltext.message:
Hello! Yes—there is an inherent tension in the text you quoted: it requires “human oversight” for high-risk AI systems and expects the assigned persons to have “necessary competence, training and authority,” while also framing AI literacy as context-dependent and (implicitly) variable in depth.

From the excerpts provided, the Act tries to resolve this in three main ways:

1) It shifts part of the burden from “understanding everything” to “designing for oversight.” Providers must identify “appropriate human oversight measures” before placing the system on the market/putting it into service, including (where appropriate) “in-built operational constraints that cannot be overridden by the system itself” and ensuring the system is “responsive to the human operator.” This reduces reliance on a human fully grasping the model’s internal complexity by embedding controllability and guardrails into the system.

2) It makes oversight a capability/role requirement, not a general expectation of all users. The text specifies that the persons assigned oversight must have the “necessary competence, training and authority” to carry out that role. Combined with AI literacy language (“adequate level of AI literacy”) and the focus on providing “knowledge necessary to understand how decisions taken with the assistance of AI will have an impact on them,” the approach is pragmatic: match literacy/competence to the task and impact rather than aiming for uniform, deep technical comprehension.

3) It relies on governance/coordination measures to raise literacy over time. The Board is described as supporting the Commission to promote AI literacy tools, public awareness, and understanding of “benefits, risks, safeguards, rights and obligations,” and Member States/Commission are to facilitate voluntary codes of conduct to advance AI literacy. This is an ecosystem approach: improve baseline capability and understanding across actors in the value chain.

Where it falls short (based on these excerpts) is that it doesn’t operationalise what “necessary” competence/training/authority or an “adequate level” of AI literacy concretely means in measurable terms, especially as system complexity increases. It also leans heavily on voluntary instruments (codes of conduct, literacy tools, awareness efforts) rather than clearly specified minimum curricula, certification, or role definitions for oversight personnel. Finally, while “in-built operational constraints” and “responsiveness to the human operator” are named as mechanisms, the excerpts don’t spell out how to assess whether oversight is truly meaningful in practice (e.g., what evidence demonstrates a human can detect and intervene in harmful behaviour rather than merely rubber-stamping outputs).
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: The Regulation requires human oversight of high-risk AI systems including that persons assigned have 'necessary competence, training and authority.' The Regulation promotes AI literacy but acknowledges notions 'may vary with regard to the relevant context.' Is there a tension between requiring human oversight of increasingly complex AI systems and the practical feasibility of meaningful human understanding? How does the Act attempt to resolve this, and where does it fall short?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
Hello! Yes—there is an inherent tension in the text you quoted: it requires “human oversight” for high-risk AI systems and expects the assigned persons to have “necessary competence, training and authority,” while also framing AI literacy as context-dependent and (implicitly) variable in depth.

From the excerpts provided, the Act tries to resolve this in three main ways:

1) It shifts part of the burden from “understanding everything” to “designing for oversight.” Providers must identify “appropriate human oversight measures” before placing the system on the market/putting it into service, including (where appropriate) “in-built operational constraints that cannot be overridden by the system itself” and ensuring the system is “responsive to the human operator.” This reduces reliance on a human fully grasping the model’s internal complexity by embedding controllability and guardrails into the system.

2) It makes oversight a capability/role requirement, not a general expectation of all users. The text specifies that the persons assigned oversight must have the “necessary competence, training and authority” to carry out that role. Combined with AI literacy language (“adequate level of AI literacy”) and the focus on providing “knowledge necessary to understand how decisions taken with the assistance of AI will have an impact on them,” the approach is pragmatic: match literacy/competence to the task and impact rather than aiming for uniform, deep technical comprehension.

3) It relies on governance/coordination measures to raise literacy over time. The Board is described as supporting the Commission to promote AI literacy tools, public awareness, and understanding of “benefits, risks, safeguards, rights and obligations,” and Member States/Commission are to facilitate voluntary codes of conduct to advance AI literacy. This is an ecosystem approach: improve baseline capability and understanding across actors in the value chain.

Where it falls short (based on these excerpts) is that it doesn’t operationalise what “necessary” competence/training/authority or an “adequate level” of AI literacy concretely means in measurable terms, especially as system complexity increases. It also leans heavily on voluntary instruments (codes of conduct, literacy tools, awareness efforts) rather than clearly specified minimum curricula, certification, or role definitions for oversight personnel. Finally, while “in-built operational constraints” and “responsiveness to the human operator” are named as mechanisms, the excerpts don’t spell out how to assess whether oversight is truly meaningful in practice (e.g., what evidence demonstrates a human can detect and intervene in harmful behaviour rather than merely rubber-stamping outputs).

Content: Text Content:
competence, in particular an adequate level of AI literacy, training and authority to properly fulfil those tasks. Those  obligations should be without prejudice to other deployer obligations in relation to high-risk AI systems under  Union or national law. (92) This Regulation is without prejudice to obligations for employers to inform or to inform and consult workers or  their representatives under Union or national law and practice, including Directive 2002/14/EC of the European  Parliament and of the Council (39),
----
  that end, appropriate human oversight measures should be identified by the provider of the system before its placing  on the market or putting into service. In particular, where appropriate, such measures should guarantee that the  system is subject to in-built operational constraints that cannot be overridden by the system itself and is responsive  to the human operator, and that the natural persons to whom human oversight has been assigned have the necessary  competence, training and authority to carry out that role. It is also essential
----
 working conditions and ultimately sustain the  consolidation, and innovation path of trustworthy AI in the Union. The European Artificial Intelligence Board (the  ‘Board’) should support the Commission, to promote AI literacy tools, public awareness and understanding of the  benefits, risks, safeguards, rights and obligations in relation to the use of AI systems. In cooperation with the relevant  stakeholders, the Commission and the Member States should facilitate the drawing up of voluntary codes of conduct  to advance AI literacy among persons
----
 knowledge necessary to understand how decisions taken with the  assistance of AI will have an impact on them. In the context of the application this Regulation, AI literacy should  provide all relevant actors in the AI value chain with the insights required to ensure the appropriate compliance and  its correct enforcement. Furthermore, the wide implementation of AI literacy measures and the introduction of  appropriate follow-up actions could contribute to improving working conditions and ultimately sustain the  consolidation, and innovation path of trustworthy AI in the Union. chunks.text: that end, appropriate human oversight measures should be identified by the provider of the system before its placing on the market or putting into service. In particular, where appropriate, such measures should guarantee that the system is subject to in-built operational constraints that cannot be overridden by the system itself and is responsive to the human operator, and that the natural persons to whom human oversight has been assigned have the necessary competence, training and authority to carry out that role. It is also essential

competence, in particular an adequate level of AI literacy, training and authority to properly fulfil those tasks. Those obligations should be without prejudice to other deployer obligations in relation to high-risk AI systems under Union or national law. (92) This Regulation is without prejudice to obligations for employers to inform or to inform and consult workers or their representatives under Union or national law and practice, including Directive 2002/14/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council (39),

working conditions and ultimately sustain the consolidation, and innovation path of trustworthy AI in the Union. The European Artificial Intelligence Board (the ‘Board’) should support the Commission, to promote AI literacy tools, public awareness and understanding of the benefits, risks, safeguards, rights and obligations in relation to the use of AI systems. In cooperation with the relevant stakeholders, the Commission and the Member States should facilitate the drawing up of voluntary codes of conduct to advance AI literacy among persons

knowledge necessary to understand how decisions taken with the assistance of AI will have an impact on them. In the context of the application this Regulation, AI literacy should provide all relevant actors in the AI value chain with the insights required to ensure the appropriate compliance and its correct enforcement. Furthermore, the wide implementation of AI literacy measures and the introduction of appropriate follow-up actions could contribute to improving working conditions and ultimately sustain the consolidation, and innovation path of trustworthy AI in the Union. The
